result.markdown:
--- title: "Get started" subtitle: "From event timing to a typed first-level fit." jupyter: python3 --- ## Install ```bash pip install fmrimod ``` For local development from this checkout: ```bash pip install -e . ``` ## The 30-Second Tour Start with an event table and a sampling frame. The event table stores experimental timing; the sampling frame defines the acquired time grid. In the full workflow, those timing objects travel with an `fmri_dataset` into `fmri_lm()`. ::: {#18125588 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import fmrimod as fm from fmrimod.design_colmap import design_colmap rng = np.random.default_rng(7) events = pd.DataFrame({ "onset": [0, 12, 24, 36], "condition": ["face", "scene", "face", "scene"], "duration": [2, 2, 2, 2], }) sf = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=40, TR=1.0) data = rng.normal(size=(40, 8)) dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(data, tr=1.0, events=events) ``` ::: Use a formula to turn those events into typed task regressors. The string formula is convenience syntax for the same design-spec tree used by the object builders. ::: {#b8a13f12 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} model = fm.event_model( 'onset ~ hrf(condition, basis="spmg1")', data=events, sampling_frame=sf, ) model.column_names ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` ['condition_condition.face', 'condition_condition.scene'] ``` ::: ::: The resulting matrix has one row per acquired sample and one column per model regressor. It is inspectable before fitting. ::: {#c9205691 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} design = model.design_matrix design.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` (40, 2) ``` ::: ::: Fit through the canonical first-level seam. The result carries coefficient matrices, residual degrees of freedom, solver configuration, and contrast methods. ::: {#ad7512ca .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} fit = fm.fmri_lm("hrf(condition)", dataset) fit.n_coefficients, fit.n_voxels ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` (3, 8) ``` ::: ::: ## What Just Happened `SamplingFrame` defines the time axis, `fmri_dataset()` binds data to timing and events, `event_model()` shows the compiled design surface, and `fmri_lm()` fits the public first-level path. This is the same architectural shape used by flagship parity workflows: ```text fmri_dataset -> fmri_lm -> contrast -> group_fit ``` You can inspect the design surface with the same top-level API: ::: {#7c15fff1 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} colmap = design_colmap(model) colmap[["col", "name", "role", "model_source"]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
col name role model_source
0 1 condition_condition.face task event
1 2 condition_condition.scene task event
``` ::: ::: ## Next Steps - [Tutorial: design matrices](tutorials/design-matrices.qmd) - [Tutorial: linear model](tutorials/linear-model.qmd) - [Tutorial: group analysis](tutorials/group-analysis.qmd) - [API reference](reference/index.qmd)
